title: Chapter 2: The Myth
content: I haven't slept.

The laptop keyboard is cold under my fingers, that particular plastic chill that only comes from hours of contact, skin oils failing to warm the keys anymore. My eyes burn. The coffee on my desk went cold two hours ago and I drank it anyway, bitter and room-temp, because getting up felt like admitting defeat. Outside, the Pacific does its endless breathing — white noise I'm already learning to tune out — and the cottage is so quiet I can hear the hard drive spinning.
Jax Vance is everywhere.

I started with TikTok. Fan edits set to synth-pop, transition after transition: Jax at a podium, Jax on a yacht, Jax laughing with her head thrown back in that way that looks choreographed even when it's candid. Then Forbes, 30 Under 30, the profile shot where she's wearing a Mugler blazer cut so sharp it could draw blood, one hand resting on a console running terminal code. Then Wired, the deep-dive piece: "Phantom Ledger and the Woman Who Built a New Architecture of Trust." Then a Netflix documentary trailer, forty seconds of moody lighting and a voiceover calling her "the most enigmatic figure in Web3."
Jax Vance. Twenty-six. Founder of Phantom Ledger, a blockchain infrastructure company valued at eight billion dollars. A unicorn by any measure, and she wears the horn like a crown.
But the deeper I dig, the stranger it gets.

Every photo is curated to the pixel. Mugler blazers paired with Marcus Aurelius quotes set in Inter font. Champagne flutes catching light beside open laptops scrolling green-on-black terminal output. One post shows her in a silk shirt, cuffs rolled, holding a dog-eared copy of *The Sovereign Individual* — but the spine isn't creased. Another: her at a conference, mid-gesture, the auditorium behind her bathed in blue light like she's delivering a sermon. The aesthetic is unmistakable: old money philosopher who controls the future. She's Gatsby with better PR and a blockchain.
The comments split clean down the middle. "queen 👑" vs. "fraud." No in-between. Nobody says "she seems fine." Nobody is neutral about Jax Vance.
I screenshot the fourth profile I've found — a fan account with two hundred thousand followers dedicated to decoding her outfit choices — and my phone buzzes. FaceTime. Kai's name fills the screen.
I answer, and his face fills the frame: dorm room lighting, a protein shake in one hand, critical expression dialed to eight.
"You look like garbage," he says, by way of hello.
"Hi to you too."
"Have you slept?"
"I'm researching."

"You're obsessing." He takes a sip of the shake. I can hear the music from his gym through the phone. "Maya. She's buying attention. The whole brand is a funnel. You post the right aesthetic, you attract the right followers, you convert them into users. It's influencer economics dressed up in blockchain language."
"I know that."
"Then why are you still awake?"

I want to agree with him. Part of me does. The part that knows better, the part that grew up watching my mother spot grifters at the temple fundraiser by their shoes — too new, too shiny, performing wealth instead of possessing it. That part of me looks at Jax Vance's feed and sees the same performance.
But another part of me — the part that's been awake since four AM, that can't stop scrolling — notices something.
"Hold on," I say.

I go back to the grid. Jax's posts, three hundred and twelve of them across four platforms. Every single one is geotagged and timestamped. She hasn't turned off location data. Either she doesn't know or she doesn't care, and nobody who curates this carefully doesn't know.
I open the metadata extractor I downloaded two hours ago and start pulling.
"Maya? You still there?"
"Shh."
The timestamps populate in my spreadsheet. One by one. Post after post. And a pattern emerges like a shape surfacing from dark water.
Every post. Every single one.

11:47 PM. 11:47 PM. 11:47 PM. The column reads like a heartbeat — the same number, over and over, dozens of times across months. Different days, different locations, different content. Always 11:47 PM.
"Kai," I say. "Nobody posts at the same minute every time unless it's scheduled."
"Or they're insane."
"Who schedules authenticity?"
He's quiet for a beat. Then: "You might actually be onto something."
The call ends. I keep pulling data.

The metadata spreadsheet grows, columns filling with latitudes and longitudes, timestamps, platform IDs, engagement metrics. My fingers ache from typing but I don't stop. The cold of the keys has migrated into my knuckles, into my wrists, and I flex them against the ache and keep going.

I find the Phantom Ledger white paper at 5:14 AM. It's a PDF, forty-seven pages, dense with cryptographic notation I can barely parse. But I read it anyway, skimming the technical architecture for anything I can understand. The consensus mechanism. The zero-knowledge proof implementation. The decentralized oracle network.
I text a friend — Eli, from the crypto beat I did last year — and ask if the tech is real.
His reply comes in under a minute: *Painfully real. Phantom Ledger's ZK-rollup implementation is better than anything Ethereum's got in production. Whoever designed this knows their shit.*
So the tech is real. The infrastructure is genuinely innovative. The product exists.
But the origin story is a black hole.

I Google "Jacqueline Vance Columbia University." Nothing relevant. I try the student directory archives. I try the yearbook database. I try the scholarship listings, the honor roll, the dean's list, the disciplinary records. Columbia's public-facing systems don't show a single hit for any variation of the name. No Jacqueline. No Jax. No J. Vance.
My hands go cold.

I check my own notes from Cliff House. Jax told me — or implied — that she was expelled from Columbia for unpaid tuition. That means she was enrolled. You can't be expelled from somewhere you never attended. But if there's no enrollment record—
The cottage feels smaller. The Pacific keeps breathing. I pull my blanket over my shoulders and stare at the screen, and the screen stares back, offering nothing.
A knock at the door.
I flinch so hard my coffee mug jumps. The dregs — cold, bitter, a ring of sediment at the bottom — slosh onto my hand and I wipe them on my jeans without looking, already on my feet.
Another knock. Three raps, unhurried. Confident.
I open the door.

Jordan Keeler leans against the doorframe like they own Malibu, which — depending on how you measure these things — they sort of do. They're tall, angular, with cheekbones that could cut glass and dark eyes that hold too much information. A vintage YSL blazer, cream with gold buttons, draped over a Nike basketball jersey. Vintage sneakers. A watch that probably costs more than my cottage's annual rent.
And they smell like tomato leaf and cedar — green and sharp and warm all at once, a cologne that costs six hundred dollars an ounce and dares you to forget it.
"Maya Chen." Their voice is unhurried, warm, the vocal equivalent of leaning back in a chair you're not worried about being asked to leave. "Two hundred thousand views in twelve hours. You're good."
"I'm a realist."
The smile that crosses Jordan's face is knife-sharp, but it's not cruel. It's the smile of someone who appreciates a good answer. "She wants to meet you properly. Tonight. There's a car at eight."
The pronoun lands. *She.* Jax.
"Why me?"
Jordan tilts their head. The afternoon light catches their cheekbones at an angle that belongs in a photography textbook. "Because you're the only person in this zip code who isn't pretending."
"That's not an answer."

"It's the only one you need." They push off the doorframe, smooth and easy, and the scent of tomato leaf and cedar lingers after they step back. "Eight o'clock. Black car. Don't be late — she hates waiting, and I hate watching people disappoint her."
Then they're gone, walking down the gravel path like the ground should feel honored, and I'm standing in my doorway with cold coffee on my hand and my heart doing something complicated.
I close the door. Lean against it. Breathe.
I have fifty-seven minutes.

I stand in front of my closet and the closet stares back with the minimalist hostility of a wardrobe that has never been equal to a billionaire's invitation. Three blouses, two of them wrinkled. A denim jacket. Two sweaters. One black turtleneck — the one I bought for my mother's birthday dinner and never wore again because she said it made me look "severe."
I pull it on.
My only pair of boots without scuffs — ankle-height, leather, beginning to crack at the left heel but passable in low light. I lace them tight.

The mirror on the back of the closet door shows me myself: thin, tired, determined. Dark circles I can't do anything about. Hair pulled back, practical. The turtleneck does make me look severe. Good. Severe is useful. Severe means I won't be distracted.

I grab my camera bag. The weight of it is familiar, grounding — the body, the lenses, the extra batteries, the lanyard strap I've replaced twice. This isn't a party. This isn't a social call. This is research. This is a photo essay that just expanded its perimeter, and I need to be ready to document whatever I find.
At 7:52 I turn back to my laptop. The metadata spreadsheet still glows on the screen, filled with numbers I've been staring at for hours. I scroll to the bottom to close it and stop.
The geotags.

I've been so focused on the timestamps that I barely registered the locations. But now, scrolling through the earliest entries — the first posts, from two years ago, before Phantom Ledger existed, before the Forbes profile and the Netflix trailer and the eight-billion-dollar valuation — the coordinates don't point to San Francisco. Not Silicon Valley. Not New York.
Two locations, alternating.
The first cluster: a neighborhood in Akron, Ohio. Residential street, modest houses, satellite view showing cracked driveways and overgrown lawns.
The second: a marina in Malibu. Slips for yachts. Salt air and teak decks.
Akron and Malibu. The Rust Belt and a yacht club. Three thousand miles apart, connected by a straight line I can't trace.

I screenshot the geotags. I email the file to myself. I close the laptop but I don't shut it down — the spreadsheet still glows through the cracks in the hinge, tinny light leaking into the darkening cottage.

I step outside. The evening air hits my face, cool and salt-heavy, carrying the sound of the surf and the distant bass thump of music from somewhere down the coast. Parked at the end of my gravel path, engine humming low and even as a heartbeat, is a black Escalade with tinted windows so dark they look like holes cut in the world.
I walk toward it.

Halfway there, I look back. The cottage is lit from within, warm yellow light spilling through the windows. On the desk, the laptop. On the screen, the data. The geotags. Akron. Malibu. Akron. Malibu.
Why would a crypto queen's earliest digital footprint start in the Rust Belt and end at a yacht club?
What connects those two dots?
The Escalade's door opens. I look forward again, climb inside, and the door closes behind me with a sound like a vault sealing shut.
